title: A very interesting old town
text: We spent three days walking in Quito, and the city really seduced us. The historic centre and the old town are a real colonial jewel, with cobblestone streets, lively squares and colourful facades. Quito, founded in 1534 on the remains of ancient Inca cities, is full of history and architecture: baroque churches such as the Compañía or San Francisco are incredible, often richly decorated and very photogenic. Local markets bring a lively and authentic touch, with crafts, fruit, and local specialties. Every corner offers a discovery, and walking allows you to feel the unique atmosphere of this city that combines heritage, culture and daily life. A real favorite for history and architecture lovers!

title: Please be aware of thieves.
text: My wife and I had one day to spend in Quito on our way to the Galapagos. We stayed on the edge of the Old Town and on Sunday morning walked a seemingly safe and obvious route from Plaza de San Francisco to Independence Square and on up to the Basilica of the National Vow. Sunny day, lots of people about but as we walked up Venezuela to the Basilica, it seemed a bird poohed on my wife from above. A seemingly helpful man carrying a water bottle stared to pour water over some of the ‘poop’. An older man seemingly just walking up the road too pointed upwards to indicate this must have been a bird. It wasn’t. They had thrown a mucky bird-poop like mixture onto my wife and while we were distracted stole the rucksack I had stupidly put on the ground against a wall - they actually swapped an empty black  rucksack for ours which meant it took us a little longer to realise we’d been robbed. We are experienced travellers and thought we were being very careful - beware of the speed and ingenuity of Quito thieves.

title: Private Guide of Quito
text: Spent a full day in Quito before we head to the Galapagos tomorrow for our Lindblad Expeditions National Geographic Expeditions cruise. Through the excellent advice and recommendations from the group we hired Ricardo Villalobos as our guide for the day. He picked us up at the Swissotel (also highly recommend) at 8:45 and for almost 9 hours took us to all the top sites on our list: the middle of the earth/equator, the Church of the Society of Jesus (golden church), Independence Square, lunch at La Vid ( fantastic!), Virgin of the Panecillo on the hill with spectacular views, chocolate shopping, La Ronda ( would only go there with a guide), and shopping at the indoor markets. Ricardo is extremely professional, knowledgeable, accommodating, seriously knows his way around the city, and went above and beyond to cover everything we wanted to see. I felt COMPLETELY safe with him and would not tour the city on my own. Traffic is crazy and neighborhoods can change quickly. His car is super clean and comfortable. And he’s extremely reasonable. He has excellent reviews on Trip Advisor which are well earned. I highly highly recommend him! Lastly, every single Ecuadorian we met was friendly, gracious and welcoming. They are lovely people!”

title: World Heritage Site
text: A world heritage site, the Historic Center of Quito preserves the colonial characteristics of the "master plan" of 1700: elongated and low houses, with patios, which extend onto the streets which, although on uneven terrain, reproduce whenever possible, a chessboard. And then the squares, with the central Plaza Major, and the churches and convents often examples of the Baroque school of Quito (Escuela Quitena), which brings together indigenous and European artistic traditions and which is renowned for having provided America's greatest contribution Spanish to universal art.I must admit that such beauty, however, did not move me deeply as it happened in other historic centers.

title: Beautiful historic center very well preserved and rich in history and art and excellent restaurants
text: Dedicate some time to this visit, better with an expert guide, so as not to miss the details of this beautiful historic center. Founded on Inca ruins, among colonial buildings and well-maintained squares, we were amazed at how clean and well-kept it was, the small balconies full of pots of geraniums were beautiful, but be careful of pickpockets and use caution in the evening. The interior of the churches is sumptuous, such as the Jesuit church of the Society of Jesus of Quito (Iglesia de la Compañía de Jesús) decorated with gold leaf ornaments, magnificent gilded ceilings, beautiful paintings and the gold-plated main altar. It took 160 years to complete Ecuador's most decorated church. The Church of San Francesco with its beautiful square and the convent are also worth visiting, as is the cathedral which overlooks the beautiful Plaza de la Independencia or the Gothic basilica, without forgetting the museums and excellent restaurants.

title: Such a special place
text: I am not surprised that Quito Old Town is in the list of the Unesco Heritage Sites. The colonial Spanish feel is all over the place with narrow streets and vast squares and the miriads of churches and convents. The walks up and down the three Main Streets (Quayaqil, Venezuela and Garcia Moreno) are full of little shops with interesting local artefacts and little restaurants and in the afternoon full of buskers and little stands of local craft and the music is coming from all over the places!Quito do not have the grandeur of other Colonial Spanish cities (like Havana or Old Panama) but I love the cosyness of the low raise buildings and the pastel colour of the houses.Certainly a wonderful discovery.

text: Old Quito combines a lot of the style of both Baroque Spain and South America.  There are several plazas and wide pedestrian roads and every corner has an ornate church or palace on it.  Quito and Ecuador are not wealthy, but downtown Quito was vibrant and people were friendly, and although it was lively, it wasn't too crowded.  I got some sense that Quito could be dangerous, or at least that theft was a problem.  But I did not feel any sense of menace while I was in Old Quito (or other downtown areas). title: Beatiful and Historic with caveats
text: Quito's Centro Historico is considered one of the best preserved and largest colonial centers in the Americas, filled with buildings that are three hundred plus years old. Ornate churches seem to dot every corner.  So many of the buildings are simply works of art. Plazas located in the center are lively filled with locals, vendors, and tourists, at least during the day. There are a number of museums one can visit, along with numerous restaurants and stores.One caveat is generally speaking at night the old town is largely deserted and feels unsafe/seedy (other than the Plaza Independencia/presidential place area and La Ronda on weekends).  Another is even during day one must be somewhat careful, we were told by many people to mind our wallets, don't keep cellphones out and keep our wits in general. There is a sizeable homeless presence with some coming relatively close begging for money, however there is a large police presence during the day, overall it felt safe. In the end nothing happened to us and we enjoyed our visit.The old town is among the highlights of Quito, and should be visited, travel in groups if you can, don't wander alone at night, just be mindful of your surroundings and you should be fine.

title: Beautiful old town, but always felt on my guard
text: Our wonderful old hotel, up on a hill, was very close by the Old Town. So we were in the historic center many times on different occasions during our stay. I was aware of safety issues, so I tried to take the necessary precautions. I never did get 'victimized' but there was at least one occasion where I could see a 'set up' taking place involving two other people. This occurred close to the entrance of Calle La Ronda. The moment I sensed it, we simply walked away briskly towards more populated areas. And we got acquainted with some other tourists that had some unfortunate experiences. One was an attempted robbery at La Ronda, which started as a 'request for payment' just to walk on the street of La Ronda. Another couple we ran into were targeted for a scam in La Carolina park. In this situation, they spray you from behind, leading you to believe a bird pooped on your, then try to get 'touchy-feely' to help clean you up, all the while pick-pocketing. Based on some reviews, it sounds like the situation is worse in post-pandemic times. Also note that operating hours for business and tourist attractions are unreliable. Don't count on websites, travel books, or locals to give you accurate information. We could not even get accurate info from the street police, or the tourist info center. For example, the 'changing of the guard' advertised in travel books is no longer taking place since the pandemic. And yet many locals told us that it was. We only knew for sure by showing up at the designated time, and nothing was happening. Note that this was my experience in the summer of 2022.

title: Beauty and history at the doorstep of the Andes.
text: Somewhat free from the restrictions imposed by the COVID pandemic, and initially scheduled for April of 2020, my wife and I finally embarked on our South American trip to Quito and the Galapagos Islands, in the beautiful country of Ecuador.Quito is a city worth seeing but we highly recommend using a guide. It will cost you about 100$ per person (if you are a couple), per day, but it will save time and frustration trying to make your way from one point of interest to another. We were immensely satisfied with the services of our guide from “Tours by Local”. Do remember that Quito is a high-altitude city of close to 2850 meters (9350ft.), so take it slow and pace yourselves. For some, higher elevations may mean greater difficulty in breathing, sleeping, headaches, or a combination of all. From old Spanish colonial and religious sites, to overseeing the city from the mountain top, tasting your way through culinary delights, and above all people that are friendly and kind, Quito has much to offer for the quasi “off-beaten-path” traveler. As Quito is no different from any other city of importance throughout the world, vigilance is recommended.  Some quarters should not be walked alone (as a couple or individually) after dark. Otherwise, have an experience of a life time.

title: A highly recommended experience of Old Town
text: I really enjoyed the experience of Old Town, Quito.  The architecture, the cobble streets, the history, food, culture... all of it was very enjoyable.  We had a guided walking tour that lasted about 3+ hours.  Our guide was incredibly knowledgable and took us to some great places.  Most people are very friendly and always smiled at me when I smiled at them.  Other than a few tasteless men cat-calling (whom I ignored), I didn't experience any negative interactions.  I was carrying around my large, Canon SLR, which did make me a little uneasy, but I was with 5 other people (including my husband).  The guide said that the pandemic has made poverty and crime worse in the city and that pick-pocketing can happen if you leave your wallet or cell-phone exposed.  He said that as long as we were keeping our valuables secure, we should be okay.  There were a couple areas he did call out as being unsafe during the night and so we should avoid.  Right now (Jan/Feb 2022), for anyone considering a visit soon, they do require you to show your vaccination card to enter many places so one good recommendation our guide gave was to take a photo of your important documents, your ID, passport, vaccination card, and to never carry them on you in case you do become a victim of theft or pick-pocketing.

title: The Heart of Quito, Colonial but Uniquely Ecuadorian
text: A UNESCO World Heritage Site - one of the first - this is the best preserved - and oldest, extant colonial city remaining in South and Central America. It is a living history - once a symbol of European hegemony over indigenous people, it now is truly Ecuadorian - mixing the colonial style with art, music, life that is distinctly modern, but unique. The architecture is incredible, and many neat little shops. Can wander around for a day or two - be sure to visit the Presidential Plaza - a central focal point of life in Quito, where locals spontaneously show up with instruments, or even microphones to sing - the life here is incredible, it is beautiful at night and most parts are well policed and safe. Avoid the edges of Old Town unless you are used to traveling in developing countries and know how to keep an eye out for potential pickpockets. Stay in the core, and you'll find it a wonderful place that may make you want to never leave - you will never find this in the US.

title: Beautiful World Heritage Site!
text: The history and charm of Old Town are an amazing way to spend a day or three wandering around. Wander into churches, taste the great chocolate made in Ecuador and stop to sit and enjoy the plazas. With that said, I want to put out a traveler's warning: my husband had his wallet stolen within the first 20 minutes we were there. Here's how it happened: we were walking/standing on the street looking around when all of a sudden it seemed we had bird poop all over us.  Immediately 2 guys came up and offered to "help us".  They had napkins to wipe it off.  As they were "helping" us, one guy lifted my husband's wallet out of his (front) pants pocket. We didn't know this happened until about a half hour later and by then they had charged nearly $5000 USD on the card.  Beware of professional pick-pocketers out there!  Don't let this alter your plans about going here, just be super careful and vigilant about anyone crowding your space. Wear a money belt or under clothing bag.

title: Multi-family Trip to Heaven :) With Gate 1 Travel and Wilo our Main Guy <3
text: Getting there! Our group of five families made a scene together on the way to Quito, capital of Ecuador.  On average, we spent 5 hours of each day on the bus except one day in Amazon. The main benefit of having a large group was having our own bus. We had a great time! The roads in Ecuador were nice, lighted and clean, many stretch of the road similar to roads heading to North in Iran! We went from altitude of 3000 to 10,000 to 600 in our travels. A few got altitude sickness but nothing stopped the show! The icing on the cake was our main guide Wilson (Wilo) Tituana fromGate 1 Travel. For the 8 nights and days, he cared for every aspect of our trip, he made sure we are all safe, he took us to fun places some of which was not part of our formal itinerary and he felt like a big brother who kindly shared his vast knowledge of Ecuador's many aspects with us. I feel like I have a good friend. Thank you Wilo for doing such an amazing job!Day 2- Visiting Colonial Quito and walk through its cobblestone streets, the 7 tons golden Basilica and the ice cream making on the way to the Middle of the World!Ecuator! Where you have one foot in Southern Hemisphere and one on the North! Very nice complex with various exhibits, nice shops and nicer coffee shop! We had lunch on our own and did I mention the coffee was really good?!Wilo bought a huge bunch of roses and he made kids and hubbies to give it to the ladies! Roses are one of the main exports for Ecuador! I don’t see roses as big and full here in the U.S.

text: We enjoyed walking through Old Town and visiting the plazas, churches and museums. There is a large police presence which I find unsettling. title: Best-preserved historic center of Latin America
text: The old town was founded in 1534 by Spanish settlers on the ruins of an Inca town. 200 years later urban architect Dionisio Alcedo y Herrera designed the original city plan, which is still intact until this day. At the time a modern plan with the pattern of a chessboard, but fitting the slopes of nearby Pichincha volcano and Itchimbia and El Panecillo hills.Not only the old colonial structures are still there, but also traditional crafts and know-how developed over centuries are preserved by the people who have been living in the old town for generations. Another reason why the old town is on the UNESCO world heritage list is the influence of the Baroque school of Quito which can be observed in the many Spanish style churches and institutions. Especially the interiors of the Church and Jesuit College of La Compañía is staggering.Cars and buses were not planned to take space in the old town. Hope that the authorities will eventually take motorized vehicles out. Now only on Sunday mornings there is no black smoke and noise, as only pedestrians and cyclists are allowed in most of the old town. Wonderful to walk around in fresh air.Keep in mind that the old town is in the 'poor' area of Quito and after 9PM almost deserted. The modern town is towards the north.

title: Fascinating area, full of great sights and packed with people
text: This is the obvious place to start a visit to Quito, with some stunning buildings - among them the Basilica and the gold-covered interior of the Iglesia de la Compania - some great squares, particularly the Plaza San Francisco and of course the hugely-popular Plaza Grande, a couple of fascinating places to visit such as the excellent and highly-informative Museo de la Ciudad, and the traditional street of old houses La Ronda. There are good eateries including some fine little cafes on the southern side of Plaza Grande (try the Dulceria Colonia). Most people are really friendly, but do watch out for a well-known street robbery trick involving a pair of people one of whom squirts or drops a liquid on your hair, which one pretends to helpfully clean off while the other makes off with your bag, purse or wallet. Sadly it happened to three people we know in the first couple of hours in the city, and it's the only real blot on what's a lovely and fascinating city to visit. The Old Town is busy but easy to walk around, with the streets all laid out in a grid pattern and most of them now pedestrianised. Great fun, but take care.

title: Sunday at leisure
text: We visited old town Quito on a late Sunday morning. The locals were milling around and older gentlemen, dressed in their Sunday finest sat on benches. The central plaza was a good place to capture photographs of a variety of people and vendors. Our tour guide frequently cautioned us to be wary of pickpockets. Being relatively crowded it was easy to see how a pickpocket could ply his trade and slip away. Not far away is the basilica and museum of San Francisco. As mass was in progress we took the back steps up to the choir loft to gaze upon one of the most ornately designed wall behind the altar I have ever seen.Well worth the visit.

title: Magnificent Colonial Architecture.
text: The reason why Quito's Old Town is listed as a UNESCO World Heritage site is that it is an extremely well-preserved example of Spanish colonial architecture. Within the vast expanse of the "Centro Historico" almost all the buildings are from the 17th, 18th or 19th centuries - there is almost nothing from the 20th century - which means that a stroll through this area is highly authentic. Another feature of Quito's Old Town is the preponderance of religious sites - churches and nunneries - devoted to female religious figures. Indeed the patron saint of Quito is Saint Mariana, who expressed her devotion to Christianity by self-flagellation and self-mutilation - there can be no more profound statement of individual faith and purity of spirit. Apart from the historical aspects, Ecuadorians are extremely friendly and welcoming. This alone is a reason to visit the country.

title: HIghly recommend
text: Quito, the capital of Ecuador, was founded in the 16th century on the ruins of an Inca city and stands at an altitude of 2,850 m. The monasteries of San Francisco and Santo Domingo, and the Church and Jesuit College of La Compañía, with their rich interiors, are pure examples of the 'Baroque school of Quito', which is a fusion of Spanish, Italian, Moorish, Flemish and indigenous art.

title: Beautiful Architecture, Amazing Churches, and Scarves for $2.50 - All the Good Stuff!
text: We spent 2 days in Old Town Quito last week, on our way to the Galapagos. For photographers, the buildings are spectacular and the people are gorgeous. Like any popular "old town," there are lots of street vendors and entertainers begging for your attention and $. We were lucky enough to visit with Latin Adventures and our guide Diego helped us hit the highlights quickly, and gave us great historical insights. The churches there are magnificent, especially the Notre Dame-inspired Basilica. If you have time, climb the stairs as far up as you can go and you'll be rewarded with a beautiful view of the city.The street vendors are mostly indigenous women who are dressed in traditional garb (including wool on hot days!), often with a baby strapped to their backs. They have stacks of scarves that make great souvenirs - we got them for 2/$5, and you can negotiate. We also found a gorgeous alpaca scarf for $10. They, and our guide, insisted that they were made in Ecuador (not China). They're really quite beautiful.We had lunch at a cool place called Cafe Del Fraile, which had a gorgeous indoor courtyard. The shrimp was great, and it was so nice to find a Diet Coke (keep in mind that you always have to ask for a glass with ice).On our last day in town we visited Old Town with another tour company and spent WAY too much time at a local chocolate factory. But we managed to visit the La Virgen del Panecillo - the magnificent statue of the Virgin Mary overlooking the city.

title: Wandering Quito's older, somewhat somnambulant quarters...
text: A 30 minute bus ride from the tourist-popular Plaza Foch area gets you to the Old Town. The streets are often on a steep incline, but level out to traverse the barrios and approach the central plaza. In light of the latter, Quito’s Fiesta de la Luz serves as a commemorative event that pays homage to the country’s history."

title: One of the best Old Town centres in South America
text: I have travelled all over South America and I must say, Quito's Old Town is the best I have seen.Firstly, it isn't actually that touristy. What I mean by that is that it is a "working town" so you see many more typical shops than tourist shops.The main square had its share of tourists however, there are a lot of locals as well. There are many retirees who dress just like they were going to work in a bank (suit, tie etc) and they come down to the square to hang out. So nice to see.There are many, many beautiful churches you can visit. Every one of them are unique and interesting.The "old town" is so large that it would take you more than a week to explore all of the streets.Without a doubt, one of my favourite in South America.

title: Worth the time
text: Given the mess created by the combination of small streets and too many vehicles, it may take awhile to finally get to the old town of Quito.   But its definitely worth it.  The architecture is a great example of colonial New World thinking.  High ceilings, ornate facades and beautiful inner courtyards reflect the old Spanish influences.  Add in the amazing number of functioning churches and it becomes obvious that the Spanish influence has evolved into its own Ecuadorian style.  Strongly recommended = the San Francisco Church during a Mass, Compania de Jesus (Jesuit) church for its gold lattice work in the ceiling and walls, and the stained glass windows in the National Basilica.Keep in mind- the Metropolitan Cathedral is actually closed on Sunday (imagine that...)

title: Unexpected gem!
text: The city of flowers and UNESCO world heritage as well as our host Any (Casa de la Gringa in La Floresta) made our time in Quito very special! The old town was definitely one of the highlights of our stay.After browsing through rather discouraging online travel reviews, we did not expect the beauty and good vibe of this unique city. We would have loved to stay longer and will definitely return to Quito and Ecuador soon! Again, we learned a lot about the past and present of the place on a great free walking tour in the old town including interesting insights on why Ecuador does not have it’s own currency (originally Sucre, nowadays USD) for almost 20 years and that they once managed to have 3 presidents in 5 month. Also, their president lives in the city centre next to the mayor, the most expensive and cheapest hotel are right next to each other and they built a new subway from the north to the south part of Quito (40km) in 6 years which will be officially opened in 2020 (check this out Canada and Germany;))!!!Despite of many political disputes and challenges Quito city centre has changed a lot especially in the last two years. If you have the chance go and enjoy the place first hand! From our perspective it beats the among tourists and backpackers much more promoted Medellín easily! We used all kinds of public transport and walked a lot and always felt save and comfortable. The people are incredible kind and supportive. Nevertheless, use common sense and be aware of your surroundings especially after sunset as you would in every other mayor city in Central and South America!

title: Incredibly beautiful architecture
text: Quito has developed on some amazing geological forms. Steep hills and deep ravines are plainly visible when one arrives by air, and on the ground the old town contains many steep narrow streets, a multitude of ornate churches, museums, small shops and business, street sellers, musicians, bars and cafes and inevitably a conflagration of traffic. Surprisingly though the air quality did not seem so polluted and our walks through the narrow streets did not result in any throat or chest problems. However, the altitude can have it's own effect and for the first day headaches may occur due to the reduction of oxygen. In spite of an undesirable reputation for street crime, Quito is patrolled by many tourist and national police which gives one a certain confidence but visitors should still be reminded to take precautions with their valuables and use some protection against the very strong overhead sunlight.

title: An easy day’s touring...
text: We loved Quito Old Town - full of life and lots of history. We were lucky enough to visit during the Festival de la Luz (National Independence celebrations) but have been back since and it’s equally fun!I’d highly recommend getting a tour guide as it’s hard to spot the right places to go - particularly if Spanish isn’t your first language - but if you’re determined to go it alone don’t miss the Big Square, and make sure you try some local Desayunos (breakfast) and Almuerzos (Lunch of the Day) in one of the many cafes. If you do fancy a tour guide, there’s a brilliant free tour from the Friends Hostel if you’re staying there.Two quick warnings: the old town is famous for its pickpockets so lock up your bag and wear it on your front (even the locals do). It’s perfectly safe and we never felt hassled, but it’s not worth taking the risk. Also, look around for a good deal on food - the places directly on the tourist hot spots tend to be much more expensive than even a few streets away.Top Tip: go on a Monday at 11am if you can, which means you can watch the changing of the Presidential Guard.

title: Old Town Quito - first World Heritage Site
text: Our guide Jose of HappyGringo took us on a long half-day tour of Quito's Old Town on our second day in Quito.  It was an informative tour of a beautiful Old Town that dates back to the 1500's, and that only includes the Spanish part, built on the ruins of the Inca city that superseded the 1000 year old city before it, the one that the Incas themselves destroyed in advance of the Spanish conquest.   The Old Town has survived earthquakes, volcanoes, war, and political change.   It is extremely photogenic, with well-maintained buildings still in use, set against the dramatic background of both the cordillera of volcanic Andean Peaks and the basin of the supervolcano of which the individual volcanoes are but discrete eruptions.  Since the Old Town is approximately the same elevation as most of Quito, around 9000 to 9500 feet above sea level, a tour of it is a good choice for a flatlander like myself to spend shortly after arriving in Quito.

title: Tour with Overseas Adventure Travel
text: Our trip began with a visit to Old Town Quito.  We were able to get a glimpse into the government building and photos next to the ceremonial guards. We watched the peaceful protest by the Amazon peoples about the over production of oil in the endangered Amazon. We walked around the square. There is little shade. We toured the beautiful Catholic cathedral. Afterward on our way to our lunch at a local Ecuadorian restaurant (hole in wall) we stopped along the beautiful cobblestone path to sample and purchase chocolates and corn nuts from a local shop. We walked a few blocks around the broader area and enjoyed a traditional Ecuadorian lunch. The weather was beautiful though very warm with little shade. Definitely wear sunscreen and a hat while in this area. It was easy to walk around as there is little vehicular traffic. If I were not part of a guided tour I would’ve enjoyed the opportunity to sit to have coffee on the edge of the main square to people watch.  As anywhere in the world,  be mindful of your purse, camera, phone, and other valuables.  I never had any problems but this is always good advice. Note: When taking photos with your cell phone be extra careful to hold it very securely with both hands.

title: Vibrant & Interesting
text: Spent a day doing a self guided walk around Quito old town with my husband. We read and heard that it's not advisable to walk around but we are so pleased we did. We came across several of the must see attractions, saw lots of local street sellers, street food and just generally enjoyed the vibrant energy of the city. We studied our maps before leaving our hotel, made sure we carried no obvious items worth steeling and kept our wits about us. There are a lot of opportunists looking to pick pockets so you have to be aware. We started to ascend the steps to the Virgin Mary monument but a local man saw us and urged us to turn back using an action of grabbing around his neck to say we might be at risk if we walked to the top. We followed his warning and went down. There are a lot of police around and there tend to be more in the busiest tourist attraction points so you're never far from help if you need it. Fortunately we didn't. There is so much to see as you walk around and the backdrop of the mountains that appear through the streets with colourful buildings every now and then sometimes shrouded in mist, other times with a hint of sunshine it's such a beautiful place, we loved it and it's only due to the crime concerns that I've not given 5/5
